item.title: Creating a Kicksecure-Gateway for VirtualBox
item.dc:creator: @secure
item.description:
The first sentence made me giggle. Yes, you are correct, and the team at Whonix (and Kicksecure) have done a wonderful job. On a personal note, I’ve encountered limitations with Tor, specifically its lack of UDP support and relatively slow network throughput.

Thank you very much for the advice, time to dig through the source code.

item.dc:creator: @secure
item.description: Personally, I was encountering this issue on my host.

item.title: Creating a Kicksecure-Gateway for VirtualBox
item.dc:creator: @arraybolt3
item.description:
Someone has tried basically this before, namely Whonix Obviously, that uses Tor rather than a VPN, but many of the concepts are likely the same.

The Whonix source code is probably a good source of info, especially the whonix-ws-network-conf, whonix-gw-network-conf, whonix-firewall, and derivative-maker repos (the last one is important since it will show you the VM configuration needed for this to work).

item.title: How to use Veracrypt in Kicksecure18?
item.dc:creator: @arraybolt3
item.description:
tc-play is in Debian’s repos, it’s basically a reimplementation of TrueCrypt/VeraCrypt, but under a permissive license that lacks the issues of the original. It seems to have been inactive for six years, but that might (?) not be a huge issue given that the project seems to generally move slowly…

github.com

GitHub - bwalex/tc-play: Free and simple TrueCrypt/VeraCrypt Implementation...

Free and simple TrueCrypt/VeraCrypt Implementation based on dm-crypt

item.title: Creating a Kicksecure-Gateway for VirtualBox
item.dc:creator: @secure
item.description:
I’m planning a project to build a Kicksecure-style gateway VM for VirtualBox that confines all network activity to a dedicated VM (similar in concept to Whonix‑Gateway).

I have some technical experience but limited networking and Debian‑hardening knowledge, and I’m willing to learn.

My plan is to publish a how‑to: a Bash script that prepares a Kicksecure VM to act as a gateway, accompanied by step‑by‑step instructions for configuring VirtualBox network adapters manually. That’s the approach I’d take.

Has anyone attempted this before, and what pitfalls or architecture‑level issues should I watch for—particularly from people familiar with Kicksecure, Whonix network isolation, or general networking?

P.S. I’d probably use Mullvad as the default VPN.

item.title: Mullvad VPN only working with UDP-over-TCP on Kicksecure 17/18
item.dc:creator: @secure
item.description: For those who care, I resolved my issue simply by turning on Local Network Sharing in the settings of the Mullvad App.

item.title: How to change rolling direction of trackpad?
item.dc:creator: @arraybolt3
item.description:
ahoy:

The system throws the ball back at me and tells me to do it myself in something called compositor, which is not found neither in applications nor in any file search I did.

Open a terminal, and run mkdir ~/.config/labwc. Then run featherpad ~/.config/labwc/rc.xml, and in the text editor window, place the following text:

<?xml version="1.0"?>
<labwc_config>
  <libinput>
    <device category="default">
      <naturalScroll>yes</naturalScroll>
    </device>
  </libinput>
</labwc_config>

Save the file, then close the FeatherPad window. Back in the terminal, run labwc --reconfigure. Your scrolling direction should now be reversed. More info about configuring labwc can be found here:

labwc.github.io

labwc

An Openbox inspired wlroots-based stacking Wayland compositor.

ahoy:

What is compositor?

You have a bunch of graphical applications you run (web browsers, file managers, text editors, etc.). Each of those applications have to display their user interface at the same time as other applications. Only one application can draw pixels on the screen at once though, so you have a “master” application that handles all of the real pixel drawing, then all of the applications you interact with directly talk to that “master” application and provide it the pixels those applications want drawn. That “master” application is a “Wayland compositor”, or simply a “compositor”. There are lots of Wayland compositors out there; the one Kicksecure includes is called “labwc”. There are some other jobs the compositor does, like displaying the title bar and borders for many apps, and forwarding your keyboard and mouse actions to the appropriate apps.

If you research it online, you may find “Wayland” being contrasted with a thing called X11. X11 is basically an older implementation of the same concept (one master program that allows multiple other programs to draw pixels at the same time).

ahoy:

Or kanshi, behind which some other settings are hidden?

Kanshi is for configuring displays (i.e. setting their resolution and telling the system where each display is in relation to the others in a multiple-monitor scenario). See:

Kicksecure – 15 May 26

Persistent configuration (kanshi) - Platform-specific Desktop Tips

kanshi is a background process that adjusts your monitor configuration as displays are plugged in and unplugged. Monitor setups are configured in one or more profiles. Kanshi will automatically detect the correct profile to apply depending on what...

item.title: How to use Veracrypt in Kicksecure18?
item.dc:creator: @arraybolt3
item.description:
ahoy:

Do I need to enable the unrestricted admin mode every time I use it?

Possibly. A number of users have reported problems with VeraCrypt on Kicksecure after the introduction of user-sysmaint-split:

Whonix Forum – 21 May 25

Whonix user isolation (user-sysmaint-split) breaks VeraCrypt workflow

Support

Hi, I’ve been using VeraCrypt for a while on a Whonix app qube. With the latest template version (Q4.2), I haven’t been able to open my encrypted drive because it asks for admin rights, which were removed. How can I make it work with the newly...

Reading time: 5 mins 🕑 Likes: 45 ❤

ahoy:

And where is their package these days anyways? I thought I downloaded it from debian repositories few years ago while still using the old non-split Kicksecure.

VeraCrypt was not accepted into the official Debian archives due to licensing concerns. See:

bugs.debian.org

#814352 - ITP: veracrypt -- Cross-platform on-the-fly encryption - Debian Bug...

unfortunately I have to reject your package.
According to [1] “(…)TrueCrypt seems to be reserving the right to sue
any licensee for copyright infringement, no matter whether they comply
with the conditions of the license or not. Based on this, our counsel
advised that above and beyond being non-free, software under this
license is not safe to use. (…)”

VeraCrypt provides DEB files for Debian 13, which may work on Kicksecure:

veracrypt.io

VeraCrypt - Free Open source disk encryption with strong security for the...

VeraCrypt is free open-source disk encryption software for Windows, Mac OS X and Linux. In case an attacker forces you to reveal the password, VeraCrypt provides plausible deniability. In contrast to file encryption, data encryption performed by...

item.title: How to write ISO image to USB stick or SD-card?
item.dc:creator: @arraybolt3
item.description:
ahoy:

Do I need to enable the unrestricted admin mode to do it?

No, you can boot into sysmaint mode instead and do it there. Sysmaint mode has full admin permissions. You will probably have to launch the ISO image burning software from a terminal in sysmaint mode.

ahoy:

Is formatting and writing ISO to USB really something that requires admin privileges?

It is. The same subsystem that allows accessing USB disks also allows accessing internal disks, so if direct disk I/O was allowed to unprivileged applications, anything could read or overwrite any file on the system, which is bad for a number of reasons. It would also allow malware to modify an external drive in such a way that it would try to hack any computer you plugged it into thereafter, by writing malicious filesystem structures to the disk.

item.title: Dracut --force --regenerate-all will give "libkmod: ERROR: kmod_module_parse_depline: ctx=0x584cfad876d0..."
item.dc:creator: @arraybolt3
item.description:
nurmagoz:

so yeah it depends on the end user which one prefers.

Not… quite. Deleting package-owned files can cause weird breakage in some instances (for instance if a package assumes a particular package-owned file exists because the package that owns it is installed). The files may also come back if the package gets reinstalled for whatever reason. Finding the right packages to remove is annoying sometimes, but the other way can break the system.

item.title: How to remove the vim editor from the system?

item.description:
Use dummy-dependency.

sudo dummy-dependency vim

item.title: Dracut --force --regenerate-all will give "libkmod: ERROR: kmod_module_parse_depline: ctx=0x584cfad876d0..."
item.dc:creator: @nurmagoz
item.description:
arraybolt3:

apt autoremove in a sysmaint session will work for that

Doesnt sadly do that.

arraybolt3:

sometimes you have to find the exact package names and uninstall them directly.

yes this would work as well like for e.g:

sudo apt remove "--purg" linux-headers-x.xx.xx+debxx-amd64

but the catch here is again you need to know which header does actually exist which need to use the previous commands for that (or similar).. so yeah it depends on the end user which one prefers.

item.title: Dracut --force --regenerate-all will give "libkmod: ERROR: kmod_module_parse_depline: ctx=0x584cfad876d0..."
item.dc:creator: @arraybolt3
item.description: You shouldn’t usually have to delete files from those locations. Uninstalling kernel-related packages is usually the right way to do things. Sometimes sudo apt autoremove in a sysmaint session will work for that, sometimes you have to find the exact package names and uninstall them directly.

item.title: Dracut --force --regenerate-all will give "libkmod: ERROR: kmod_module_parse_depline: ctx=0x584cfad876d0..."
item.dc:creator: @nurmagoz
item.description:
Just found out that there is stupid mismatch between kernel and/or headers and/or modules, some are .74 (old default kernel) and some are .88 (latest kernel), so how to fix that:

  • Check the current kernel installed:

uname -r

  • Check and Remove old images which are for previous version/s

** Check in modules directory:

ls -1 /lib/modules

Remove old versions:

sudo rm -rf /lib/modules/x.xx.xx+debxx-amd64

** Check in headers directory:

sudo ls -1 /usr/src

Remove Old versions:

sudo rm -rf /usr/src/linux-headers-x.xx.xx+debxx-amd64

** Check in boot directory

sudo ls -lh /boot

Remove old versions:

sudo rm -f /boot/vmlinuz-x.xx.xx+debxx-amd64

and

sudo rm -f /boot/initrd.img-x.xx.xx+debxx-amd64

and

sudo rm -f /boot/initrd.img-x.xx.xx+debxx-amd64

  • Regenerate dracut and Grub

** Dracut

sudo dracut --force --regenerate-all

** Grub

sudo update-grub

Error messages should be cleaned, preferable to reboot after that:

sudo reboot

Done.

item.title: External monitor control and keyboard layout widget
item.dc:creator: @arraybolt3
item.description:
SpecialBook:

I connect an external monitor to my laptop, how do I turn only the external monitor on and the laptop screen off?

Can be configured for the current session using wdisplays (pre-installed, click Start Menu → Preferences → Displays), or persistently using kanshi (also pre-installed). See:

Kicksecure – 15 May 26

Persistent configuration (kanshi) - Platform-specific Desktop Tips

kanshi is a background process that adjusts your monitor configuration as displays are plugged in and unplugged. Monitor setups are configured in one or more profiles. Kanshi will automatically detect the correct profile to apply depending on what...

SpecialBook:

Second question: how to add a widget to display the language of the current layout? kbindicator widget is not added to the panel

Doesn’t seem like LXQt/labwc has this feature yet. Some workaround ideas are here:

GitHub

Get current keyboard layout · labwc labwc · Discussion #951

Is there any way to get the current keyborad layout(I've set layouts in environment and toggle them with alt_shift)? I searched about it and find only a way which works only in sway with swahmsg.

item.title: Kicksecure on hardware - sdwdate clock always broken on direct hard drive
item.dc:creator: @nurmagoz
item.description: Yep saw this behavior, something is lacking sdwdate from connecting properly. But more importantly is the issue above, where first time fresh installation sdwdate has clock issue which will never be fixed unless handled manually.

item.title: Vboxdrv.service fails to start
item.dc:creator: @arraybolt3
item.description:
While waiting for Oracle to publish a new version of VirtualBox with the issue fixed, some workarounds are documented here:

Recent versions of VirtualBox (7.1.18, 7.2.8) can cause a system crash during package upgrades, VMs may fail to start Support
Note that the issue described here is a VirtualBox bug, it is not a Kicksecure bug. If you use virtualbox-installer-cli to install VirtualBox, you will get a recent version of VirtualBox from Oracle’s repository. VirtualBox 7.1.18 and VirtualBox 7.2.6 (the two latest versions at the time of this writing) both have a bug that results in a kernel oops when the vboxdrv kernel module is loaded if a particular hardening setting (kernel.kptr_restrict=2) is enabled. This setting is enabled by default …

item.title: Vboxdrv.service fails to start
item.dc:creator: @Patrick
item.description:
Never mind systemcheck. It’s just the messenger and the messenger is right.

vboxdrv / VirtualBox is the issue.

item.title: Vboxdrv.service fails to start
item.dc:creator: @Patrick
item.description:
This one applicable?

github.com/VirtualBox/virtualbox

[Bug]: vboxdrv triggers a kernel BUG during load and all VMs fail to start if `kernel.kptr_restrict=2` sysctl is set

opened 01:47AM - 23 Apr 26 UTC
ArrayBolt3
bug fixed in svn

### Version 7.1.18 ### Host OS Type Linux ### Host OS name + version Debian… 13.4 ### Host Architecture x86 ### Guest OS Type all ### Guest Architecture x86 ### Guest OS name + version N/A ### Component VMM ### What happened? (Apologies if I have the component wrong, I wasn't sure whether to pick VMM or Host Support.) If a Debian system has the `kernel.kptr_restrict=2` sysctl set, VBoxDrv will trigger a kernel BUG on load. `vboxdrv.service` will show as failed in the output of `systemctl`. The module will technically be loaded into the kernel, but it is completely non-functional in this state, attempting to boot a VM always fails. If the user is unlucky enough to also have panic-on-oops enabled on their machine, this will result in the entire machine crashing. The relevant output from `dmesg` is: ``` [ 4.956947] vboxdrv: loading out-of-tree module taints kernel. [ 4.956953] vboxdrv: module verification failed: signature and/or required key missing - tainting kernel [ 4.968023] vboxdrv: Found 8 processor cores/threads [ 4.974793] RTR0DbgKrnlInfoOpen: /proc/kallsym method failed. get_user_pages address off by +0x74a91580 [ 5.006678] Missing ENDBR: kallsyms_lookup_name+0x0/0xd0 [ 5.007463] ------------[ cut here ]------------ [ 5.007464] kernel BUG at arch/x86/kernel/cet.c:132! [ 5.008234] Oops: invalid opcode: 0000 [#1] PREEMPT SMP NOPTI [ 5.009003] CPU: 2 UID: 0 PID: 1232 Comm: modprobe Tainted: G OE 6.12.74+deb13+1-amd64 #1 Debian 6.12.74-2 [ 5.009793] Tainted: [O]=OOT_MODULE, [E]=UNSIGNED_MODULE [ 5.010566] Hardware name: Notebook NV4XMB,ME,MZ/NV4XMB,ME,MZ, BIOS 1.07.13NMIN29 07/12/2021 [ 5.011434] RIP: 0010:exc_control_protection+0x29f/0x2b0 [ 5.012312] Code: d8 b9 09 00 00 00 48 8b 93 80 00 00 00 be 80 00 00 00 48 c7 c7 1a 6b 6e 8c e8 fd a9 3c ff 80 a3 8a 00 00 00 fb e9 38 fe ff ff <0f> 0b 66 2e 0f 1f 84 00 00 00 00 00 0f 1f 44 00 00 90 90 90 90 90 [ 5.013215] RSP: 0018:ffffd523c163b8c8 EFLAGS: 00010002 [ 5.014115] RAX: 000000000000002c RBX: ffffd523c163b8f8 RCX: 0000000000000000 [ 5.015024] RDX: 0000000000000000 RSI: ffff8f2451321780 RDI: ffff8f2451321780 [ 5.015936] RBP: 0000000000000000 R08: 0000000000000000 R09: ffffd523c163b758 [ 5.016841] R10: ffffffff8ceb43c8 R11: 0000000000000003 R12: 0000000000000003 [ 5.017737] R13: 0000000000000000 R14: 0000000000000000 R15: 0000000000000000 [ 5.018648] FS: 00007fbc4d69f5c0(0000) GS:ffff8f2451300000(0000) knlGS:0000000000000000 [ 5.019557] CS: 0010 DS: 0000 ES: 0000 CR0: 0000000080050033 [ 5.020460] CR2: 00007fbc4cef3cb0 CR3: 000000010d6ca005 CR4: 0000000000f72ef0 [ 5.021364] PKRU: 55555554 [ 5.022275] Call Trace: [ 5.023183] <TASK> [ 5.024070] asm_exc_control_protection+0x26/0x30 [ 5.024964] RIP: 0010:kallsyms_lookup_name+0x0/0xd0 [ 5.025844] Code: 79 0a 48 f7 d0 48 03 05 3e d9 29 01 c3 cc cc cc cc 66 0f 1f 84 00 00 00 00 00 90 90 90 90 90 90 90 90 90 90 90 90 90 90 90 90 <66> 0f 1f 00 0f 1f 44 00 00 53 48 83 ec 10 65 48 8b 04 25 28 00 00 [ 5.026768] RSP: 0018:ffffd523c163b9a0 EFLAGS: 00010282 [ 5.027690] RAX: ffffffff8b3c7c40 RBX: ffff8f1ccd908010 RCX: 0100000000000000 [ 5.028613] RDX: 00ffffffffdfffff RSI: 0000000000000000 RDI: ffffffffc243b6a7 [ 5.029529] RBP: ffffd523c163b9f8 R08: 0000000000000000 R09: 0000000000000001 [ 5.030445] R10: 0000000000000002 R11: 0000000000000001 R12: ffffd523c163ba08 [ 5.031362] R13: ffffffffc243b6a7 R14: 0000000000000000 R15: ffff8f1ccaca1368 [ 5.032275] ? RDX: 0000000000000000 RSI: ffff8f2451321780 RDI: ffff8f2451321780 [ 5.317860] RBP: 0000000000000000 R08: 0000000000000000 R09: ffffd523c163b758 [ 5.319866] R10: ffffffff8ceb43c8 R11: 0000000000000003 R12: 0000000000000003 [ 5.321883] R13: 0000000000000000 R14: 0000000000000000 R15: 0000000000000000 [ 5.323901] FS: 00007fbc4d69f5c0(0000) GS:ffff8f2451300000(0000) knlGS:0000000000000000 [ 5.325902] CS: 0010 DS: 0000 ES: 0000 CR0: 0000000080050033 [ 5.327892] CR2: 00007fbc4cef3cb0 CR3: 000000010d6ca005 CR4: 0000000000f72ef0 [ 5.329912] PKRU: 55555554 [ 5.331911] note: modprobe[1232] exited with irqs disabled ``` The following window appears when trying to launch a VM in this state: ``` VirtualBox - Error In suplibOsInit VERR_VM_DRIVER_OPEN_ERROR (rc=-1911) The VirtualBox Linux kernel driver is either not loaded or not set up correctly. Please try setting it up again by executing '/sbin/vboxconfig' as root. If your system has EFI Secure Boot enabled you may also need to sign the kernel modules (vboxdrv, vboxnetflt, vboxnetadp, vboxpci) before you can load them. Please see your Linux system's documentation for more information. where: suplibOsInit what: 3 VERR_VM_DRIVER_OPEN_ERROR (-1911) - Was not able to open the support driver. Generic open error used when none of the other ones fit. ``` ### How can we reproduce this? 1. Install a fresh copy of Debian 13.4 with the GNOME desktop on a physical device. (I'm using a laptop with an i5-1135G7 processor.) 2. Install VirtualBox 7.1 following the "Debian-based Linux distributions" instructions from https://www.virtualbox.org/wiki/Linux_Downloads. 3. Run `sudo modprobe -r kvm_intel; sudo modprobe -r kvm` to make sure KVM doesn't get in the way of VirtualBox. 4. Launch VirtualBox, and create a FreeDOS virtual machine. 5. Verify that you can boot the VM successfully. 6. Create a file at `/usr/lib/sysctl.d/990-crash-vbox.conf`, with the contents `kernel.kptr_restrict=2`. 7. Reboot the device. 8. Run `systemctl status vboxdrv.service`. Observe that it shows as `Active: failed`. 9. Run `sudo dmesg`. You will see a kernel backtrace. 10. Run `sudo modprobe -r kvm_intel; sudo modprobe -r kvm` to get KVM to go away again. 11. Launch VirtualBox, and attempt to launch the FreeDOS virtual machine. The window described above will appear and the VM will not launch. ### Did you upload *all* of your necessary log files, screenshots, etc.? - [x] Yes, I've uploaded all pertinent files to this issue.

item.title: Vboxdrv.service fails to start
item.dc:creator: @ValdemarJo
item.description:
Hello! Help fix the problem, after the update there was an error and the virtualbox stopped working

degraded
zsh: exit 1 sudo systemctl --wait is-system-running
[user ~]% sudo systemctl --state=failed,activating,deactivating list-units
UNIT LOAD ACTIVE SUB DESCRIPTION
● vboxdrv.service loaded failed failed VirtualBox Linux kernel module

Legend: LOAD → Reflects whether the unit definition was properly loaded.
ACTIVE → The high-level unit activation state, i.e. generalization of SUB.
SUB → The low-level unit activation state, values depend on unit type.

1 loaded units listed.
[user ~]%

item.title: How do you turn on thunderbolt?
item.dc:creator: @arraybolt3
item.description:
SpecialBook:

How to disable the system setting blocking thunderbolt?

Edit /etc/modprobe.d/30_security-misc_disable.conf, then look for the following block in the file:

## Thunderbolt:
## Disable Thunderbolt modules to prevent certain DMA attacks.
##
## https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thunderbolt_(interface)#Security_vulnerabilities
##
install intel-wmi-thunderbolt /usr/bin/disabled-thunderbolt-by-security-misc
install thunderbolt /usr/bin/disabled-thunderbolt-by-security-misc
install thunderbolt_net /usr/bin/disabled-thunderbolt-by-security-misc

Comment out the three lines starting with install by adding a # character to the start of each line. Save your changes to the file, then reboot.

SpecialBook:

What are the risks of enabling this setting considering I have a modern 2026 laptop with the latest bios update

Unsure. It may depend on your specific hardware and hardware vendor (i.e. does the hardware’s IOMMU work right), and there may be unknown vulnerabilities in Thunderbolt in general. Thunderbolt (interface) - Wikipedia has a list of some known issues, which may or may not be mitigated on various machines. (Suffice to say, exposing a PCIe interface on a USB port has proven to be somewhat of a bad idea.)

FWIW, I have a USB-C SSD enclosure that does not use Thunderbolt. It works without issues even when plugged into a Thunderbolt port. It obviously isn’t as fast, but it’s likely less of a security hazard.

item.title: How do you turn on thunderbolt?
item.dc:creator: @SpecialBook
item.description:
Using USB4/Thunderbolt, I connect an external SSD drive to a laptop, but it is not detected in the system, and is determined via USB A.
In another linux distro on this laptop TB4 works.

How to disable the system setting blocking thunderbolt?
What are the risks of enabling this setting considering I have a modern 2026 laptop with the latest bios update

item.title: The CUPS and Printing Support in Kicksecure
item.dc:creator: @arraybolt3
item.description:
anonx230:

I’m trying to reduce my reliance on virtual machines for security.

I’d encourage you to not do that Kicksecure is very hardened in comparison to vanilla Debian, but even so, vulnerabilities can (and have) existed in system components like the Linux kernel that Kicksecure was vulnerable to, and where no major Linux distribution could have reasonably been immune to the vuln before it was found. (The recent Copy Fail and Dirty Frag vulns are good examples.) Using Kicksecure as a hardened virtual machine host is good defense-in-depth.

item.title: Adding Librewolf to Browser Choice for I2P Use?
item.dc:creator: @anonx230
item.description: I dont use i2p but would like to voice my support for Librewolf

item.title: Kicksecure on hardware - sdwdate clock always broken on direct hard drive
item.dc:creator: @anonx230
item.description:
nurmagoz:

Didnt understand the question, i boot into sysmaint (persistent) if thats what you mean.

Sryy what I meant is switching between modes. The behavior I noticed is the syncing of time between modes. Thus far live mode is what I use more. Time sync fails in live mode more then persistent. If I sync in persistent first before switching I don’t see a failure in sdwdate the next time I boot in live

item.title: The CUPS and Printing Support in Kicksecure
item.dc:creator: @anonx230
item.description:
arraybolt3:

If you need to print but are also concerned about the security implications, you might be able to install a dedicated Kicksecure or Debian 13 virtual machine with CUPS installed within, then pass through your USB printer to the VM and print from there.

I’m trying to reduce my reliance on virtual machines for security. The reason I’m trying Kicksecure you know is partly this reason to do most on a secure host. Since you suggested it, I might give er a go and see what its like. Wish me luck

item.title: The CUPS and Printing Support in Kicksecure
item.dc:creator: @anonx230
item.description:
Just looked through manpages and online documentation for cups. I take it CUPS does not natively support a .d/ drop-in directory structure (like /etc/cups/cups.d/`) right​

To close the listening port you have to edit the /etc/cups/cupsd.conf directly I guess and that nothing is changed after updates.

This GitHub issue specifically addresses the removal and requested reinstatement of the Include directive in cupsd.conf. A feature that would allow one configuration file to reference another.

Ok, this is for apple but looks like it could relate here. Uncertain if a .d/ drop in directory support ever existed at all

arraybolt3:

I have a physical hardware Kicksecure machine that does not have these issues.

No idea, but its reproducible from my end.

item.dc:creator: @nurmagoz
item.description:
There are PCs were they have the option to boot with dGPU (e.g Nvidia) directly from the BIOS (called Discrete mode) instead of iGPU (CPU) called Hybrid Mode from BIOS.

This is useful for better display graphic and graphics related work.

May 14 14:06:43 localhost (sd-pam)[2042]: pam_succeed_if(systemd-user:session): requirement "uid ne 0" was met by user "sysmaint"
lines 108-141/141 (END)

So there are flags need to be added to make it compatible with discrete mode:

sudo nano /etc/modprobe.d/nvidia-drm.conf

add:

options nvidia-drm modeset=1 fbdev=1

Then regenerate:

sudo dracut --force --regenerate-all

The next reboot will lead to kernel panic, only way to bootup again it remove the flags from grub:

Press e on grub window, search for the line starting with “linux”, and add to the end of it:

rd.driver.blacklist=nvidia,nvidia_drm,nvidia_modeset,nvidia_uvm modprobe.blacklist=nvidia,nvidia_drm,nvidia_modeset,nvidia_uvm module_blacklist=nvidia,nvidia_drm,nvidia_modeset,nvidia_uvm systemd.unit=multi-user.target

Then F10.

After that it will boot up, and you need to remove the added flags and regenerate dracut.

Note: I have tested that with Debian 13 DVD image with LXQT (default going to be initramfs and xorg) + Nvidia setup as before = it will bootup normally without issues.

item.title: Copy.fail / CVE-2026-31431: Linux kernel local privilege escalation vulnerability and help wanted
item.dc:creator: @FranklyFlawless
item.description:
One more:

github.com

pocs/fragnesia at main · v12-security/pocs

poc it like it's hot. Contribute to v12-security/pocs development by creating an account on GitHub.

Small quote:

Fragnesia is a universal Linux local privilege escalation exploit, discovered with V12 by William Bowling with the V12 team. Fragnesia is a member of the Dirty Frag vulnerability class. This is a separate bug in the ESP/XFRM from dirtyfrag which has received its own patch. However, it is in the same surface and the mitigation is the same as for dirtyfrag.

  • Fragnesia Linux kernel local privilege escalation vulnerability mitigations | Ubuntu
  • https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2026-46300

item.title: Kicksecure on hardware - sdwdate clock always broken on direct hard drive
item.dc:creator: @nurmagoz
item.description: Try installing KS on external flash/hard drive fresh installation (default settings), should give you the same issue when first booting into sysmaint after installation.

item.title: The CUPS and Printing Support in Kicksecure
item.dc:creator: @arraybolt3
item.description:
It’s not entirely clear to me what “CUPS with hardening and isolation” would look like. Applying special configuration to CUPS would be likely undesirable in Kicksecure because it would diverge from upstream, in a way that would potentially incur greater maintenance effort. See:

Kicksecure – 29 Apr 26

Relationship With Upstream

Kicksecure Relationship with Upstream Projects.

If you need to print but are also concerned about the security implications, you might be able to install a dedicated Kicksecure or Debian 13 virtual machine with CUPS installed within, then pass through your USB printer to the VM and print from there.
